speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you're on the death march. You're on your way to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Dachau. And when we were striked and bombed by the American mustangs, the squad leader finally realized what this-- what has been happening. So they stopped shooting. And he flew very, very low with a white handkerchief and-- and to apologize for what he-- what they have done. And we proceeded. However, before we proceeded, there was like a mass grave that we dug. And we buried all the bodies there. And-- and when it got very close to closing the grave, they-- they start killing the people, the ones that were putting the-- covering the grave up. And I was one of the people working on the graves, because I tried to run away. That was my punishment. However, there was-- an order was given by an officer to stop everything, and to evacuate immediately. And we proceeded into Dachau. So I was very lucky that I was at the other end, because a lot of people were just shot-- one bullet in the hat and they pushed them into the grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at Dachau, it was a horrible place. There were 32,000 prisoners in Dachau. We were squeezed together like herring, lying on the bunks. And the conditions were very bad. And people were very sick and very tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, there was a transport taken out of Dachau, because the crematoriums could not keep up with the mass graves, mass destruction. And-- and they would take people into the mountains in-- in Bavaria and bring all those bodies back that were froze and starved. And-- and Dachau was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. I'm a little confused. I don't quite understand this about Dachau. You said they took bodies to the mountains. But then--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took people, human beings. And stuck them into the open cars, rail cars. And they took them to the-- to the hills-- to the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: To die. And they brought them back frozen, dead. And a lot of people died this way too, because in Dachau-- when Dachau was liberated, there was mountains of bodies that were piled like you would pile rock, mountains of rock or bags, or whatever. Like pyramids of bodies in Dachau. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work while you were in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't. And they give you a piece of bread and send you on the transport. And so Jack and I, my cousin and I, we were already old hands at it. So we used to get in line and get the bread, and bring it back to the other guys. And we were very lucky that we were able to do this. So we got a little bit more food. And then when the-- the American--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks. And then we were liberated four days after my 18th birthday. That was some birthday-- April 29. And they made another mistake, because they opened up all the supplies of food and gave it to these people that were skinny. Their stomach shrank, and they-- and all ate rich food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lost a few thousand people the first week. Then they realized what was happening. So they shut the supplies down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and they were-- I went to see an officer to tell him that I want to join the army, and I want to go fight the Germans. And so he says, pick up the rifle. I couldn't pick up the rifle. I weighed 86 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, son, you've done enough fighting. And before I knew it, he called the-- the corpsman-- the medical corpsman. And he probably saved my life, because I got shots for typhoid, for all the diseases right away. And he says, feed this young man and let him go. So and we were in Dachau, my-- one of the group, one of the old friends, you know Leo Honigstein, who is now my brother-in-law-- he married my sister, Regina after the war-- he was terribly sick and with typhoid fever. And had horrible, high temperature. He was in constant delirium. He was delirious constantly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finally walked out of Dachau. We walked into Munich. And we went to Adolf Hitler Platz, the seven of us. Well, Adolf Hitler house-- Adolf Hitler's house, because it became the-- the American army made their headquarters there. So we said, we have no place to go. We want to go to Adolf Hitler Platz, house, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the sergeant said, you can't come in here, because this is the headquarters of the American army. And we were still in the prisoner uniform. But the captain was sitting there. And he says, who are these men? He says, they just came out from Dachau, they say. He says, bring them in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, take him and let him take a bath. Give him clothes, and give him some food. Well they took us down to the cellar. And my God, I-- my eyes. I saw the oranges, the apples, and the fruit, and the food. I went crazy. And that's how we went-- we got out of Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about-- when did you find out that your sisters and brother were alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was this constant turmoil where people were asking of other people. I found out that they were alive, and they are in [NON-ENGLISH], which is outside of Breslau, probably not far from [NON-ENGLISH] area. And so I said, OK. But there were no planes, no trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got together a suitcase of American whiskey, cigarettes, and all sorts of things that I could-- and I was just stopping the locomotives that would be a working locomotive, fixed railroad train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went across the border in Schwarzwald, the Black Forest from-- from Munich, from Bavaria to Austria. And then I walked myself down through Czechoslovakia. The [NON-ENGLISH], the Russians, that was the border that the Russians said, where is your-- you have no identification. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you walking to your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Black Forest. And then I was stopping convoys of Russian soldiers with American cigarettes, and get hitchhiked.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get to your brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I'm just trying-- that's what you were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I-- someone said, they are in-- but one of my sisters, actually she went to Buchenwald. And from Buchenwald, they sent her already to Sweden for rehabilitation. But the others were in this small community outside of Breslau. And I saw my brother. And everyone told him that Mark was killed in Auschwitz-- my brother, Herman. But Romek is alive, because he was four years or so older than I was. And when he saw me, he passed out, because all this time he knew that I was dead. And that's how we were reunited. And there were some cousins. And we went on a transport, after the war mind you, that they were re-expatriating the volksdeutsche-- you know, the Germans that didn't want to stay in Poland, because they will be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we became volksdeutsche. And we went straight to Munich from-- it took us a few days to get there. Also in cattle car, but it was a different kind of a transport. And that's how we all arrived in Munich. Because we're running very short of time, which I apologize, and I'm sorry for, I think we'll do things a little differently than we normally do. Why don't you tell me first when you came to the United States? I came to the United States in December 23rd, 1949. And you met and married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I served in the army maybe two years between 1952 and '54. The United States Army, I was drafted. And then after I got out of the Army two years later-- I met my-- a year later-- I met my lovely wife. And then a year later, we were married. We were engaged-- it was a long engagement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rhona-- Rhona Fiedler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was American-born.

speaker: interviewee
text: American-born in Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Los Angeles in-- about a year later, a year and a half later. She was pregnant with Shelly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have four children, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think in order to save some time, what we'll do is, when we show the photographs of your children, which we're going to show right away, then you'll tell us everybody's name and for whom they were named. I'm just trying to make it so we can get everything on this reel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why certainly, certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to rush you this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry that it took so long. I'm awfully sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So I think that concludes. We're going to bring your wife out first. And then we're going to film your photographs. You stay, and we're going to shut. So one moment. OK. Mr. Bader, I see you have a lovely woman sitting here next to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife Rhona Elaine. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: This March, coming March 18th, will be 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Bader, is there anything that you would like to say, having been married to this wonderful man now for almost 40 years and being the wife of a survivor? Anything you've learned that you'd like to pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes people ask me how I came to marry a survivor. And I always tell them that I'm married a handsome, charming, loving man named Mark Bader who happens to be a survivor. They come in all different sizes and shapes. This one happens to be very special. And we love each other very much. So I guess that's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, Mr. Bader, is there anything that you want to pass on to your children and grandchildren about your experiences from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My advice to my children is that they should cherish freedom, because it's precious. And they should not take anything for granted, because the Holocaust can repeat itself. In fact, it repeated itself in Bosnia-Herzegovina. And to always stay involved, and be cognizant of things. We have people that are running for president of the United States. And one of the gentleman happens to claim that the-- the Holocaust never happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my advice to my children is not to take anything for granted, to be involved, and to stay involved to make sure that these things will never happen again. And I know that you have some children and some grandchildren, and we're going to see their photographs next plus some photographs you have of your own family from before the war. I want to take this opportunity to thank you very much, Mr. Bader, for giving us so much of your time today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I'd like to thank you-- both of you-- very, very much for taking so much time and to putting up with my laughter, and my smiling, and cries, and sadness. I want to thank you very much for doing such a wonderful job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did it. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at approximately 1910, approximately. And right to left is my aunt, Rose, my grandfather, Jacob Bader, and my aunt, Dorothy. This was taken in Wadowice, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rose was your father's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose was my father's sister, that's right. And Dorothy was married to Solomon Bader, the oldest son of Jacob-- Jacob's oldest son. This is my grandmother, Tamara Bader. This picture was also taken in Wadowice, Poland at approximately the same time as the other pictures, around 1910 or so. This is my mother, Rachel Bader. Her maiden name was Enoch. This picture was taken approximately before World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in the city of Oswiecim, country of Poland. This is my father, Isaac Bader. This picture was taken during World War I. He was an officer in the Austrian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think this was taken in Oswiecim?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken somewhere on the battlefield in Eastern Europe. And this picture is my, from right to left, my sister-in-law, Ida Bader. My sister's sitting, Regina Honigstein, that-- and top-- above Regina is Jacob Rosenbaum, who was with me during the entire time in the various concentration camps, and myself. This was taken in approximately 1945, after the war, in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister is now married to Leo Honigstein, who we spent together since [NON-ENGLISH], throughout the rest of the concentration camps. This picture I am standing. My brother-in-law, David Chapelle is sitting. This was taken after the war, 1949 in Munich, Germany, English Tiergarten. This American soldier looks like me. This was taken during the Korean War when I was in the army, approximately 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark Bader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was corporal. And this picture is my lovely wife and myself. This picture was taken just after we were engaged in 1956 in Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us both your names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mark Bader. And my lovely wife is Rhona Elaine Bader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Rhona Elaine Fiedler. This is my grandfather's house in Auschwitz, or Oswiecim, Poland. And this was a postcard, which was blown up after my sister brought it back from one of the trips to my hometown. And it's on the main square in Oswiecim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it still there? Is the house still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still there. At the present time, it's the-- the city hall for Oswiecim. This is a picture, from right to left, is Tim Freund, and Tamara Freund-- my daughter Tamara Freund. Tamara was named after Tamara Bader, my grandmother. Next to her is Debbie-- Deborah Hope. Deborah was named after one of my wife's aunts and one of my aunts. And then I am standing next to Debbie, my wife is to the left of me. And then we have Rochelle Susan, my oldest daughter. And my youngest child, David Joseph Bader.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom are Rochelle and David named?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle was named after my youngest aunt, Rochelle Susan Enoch. And David was named after-- my mother-- my wife's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife is shaking her head no.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, let's stop. Your wife is shaking her head no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle was named for your mother. Rochelle is your mother's name. Susan was your aunt's.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter, Deborah, is named for my grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I make a correction?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's already made. It's made.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got confused. And I don't have my glasses on. Next to my wife is standing Rochelle Susan, who was named after my mother and my aunt, my mother's younger sister. Next to her is David Joe, actually David Joseph Bader. He's named after my wife's grandfather and my uncle, the oldest uncle, Enoch-- Joseph Enoch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in July of 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and state?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles. Actually Beverly Hills.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my grandchildren, from right to left, is Daniel Jordan. He was named after my wife's grandfather and my Grandfather Bader. Next to him is Joshua Henry. He was named after my youngest brother, Joshua. And Henry after my wife's father. Next to Joshua is Hannah Linton Freund. Hannah was named after my aunt Helen, who I stayed with when I came to this country in Detroit, Michigan. And she sent me the papers to come to this country. And Linton is a name from the Freund's family. Next to her is Andrea Carol Goodman. Andrea was named after her father's family names. And sitting on Andrea's lap is [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Adam. Adam Bader Freund. He is our youngest grandchild. He is-- he was named after my brother, [NON-ENGLISH], who did not survive the camp-- the camps. And after, they left the Bader family.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the last picture. When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken at Passover of 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Regency Club in Westwood, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the last photograph. It's so nice to be able to end on your grandchildren and to know for whom they've all been named. I want to thank you very, very much for everything today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very, very much. And also I'd like to thank Mr. Spielberg for his-- his generosity that he was able to create this. I am very impressed with what he has done, and what he is still trying to accomplish.
